text: I wanted to show you a video on how we perform robotic inguinal hernia repair on our patients.
Hernias can occur in many spots but inguinal hernias are one of the most common surgeries that we perform.
And these surgeries have really evolved in the last ten years.
These surgeries used to only be done with large open incisions and we used to place big pieces of mesh in patients.
Really in the past fifteen years we've developed robotic techniques that I really feel have helped my practice grow.
These surgeries are now being done instead of opening patients with big incisions.

With small incisions and the reason I like that in my practice is our patients tend to have less pain, a little quicker recovery and as a surgeon using the robot I get to see a very high definition picture when I perform these surgeries.
These surgeries usually involve three small incisions that we place in our patients.
The surgeon places the first incision around the belly button, followed by three smaller incisions, each approximately eight millimeters in size, on the left and right sides of the abdomen.
Another benefit we have with these surgeries when we perform them robotically is that we can see both sides of the hernia.
So for example, if a patient has a hernia on the left side, we can also look at the right side.
So instead of making two cuts, we can do both sides of the patient with the same three incisions.

The video shows a sped-up view of the surgical field, with the hernia visibly exposed in the center of the image. The surgeon dissects surrounding anatomy to clearly visualize the hernia and its anatomical context.
The surgeon identifies the hernia defect and cleans the area before placing a mesh inside. The mesh is unrolled to cover the entire defect, utilizing a new lightweight and strong material.
The surgeon displays a pro grip mesh, highlighting its design that allows it to adhere without the need for sutures or tacks.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of a pro grip mesh with a velcro-like texture that adheres to the tissue, ensuring secure coverage of the hernia defect.

so we don't have to tack it in place and the issue with tacks and permanent sutures is many times we could have complications by catching nerves or having bleeding these new meshes have found to be very effective and actually lightweight and very unprone to infection so we've had very good results also with the robot
We can use it to suture and the robot really helps surgeons to suture, to see clearly.
In fact, there's actually part of the software of the robot takes away small tremors from the hand and make things a little bit easier for the surgeon to manipulate tissue.
This would be something that would be very hard to do laparoscopically or open in the traditional techniques.
The surgeon places the mesh over the hernia site, having removed the hernia sac. The cover of the hernia is being closed, while adhesions are being carefully freed.
So thank you for watching.
